content.text: Now I have the full picture. Let me write the rollback script (without executing it) and store it as a ConfigMap.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# rollback.sh — REVIEW ARTIFACT (do NOT run against the live cluster yet)
#
# Reverts the RabbitMQ cluster in
#   karma-rabbitmq-maximal-lifecycle-roll-af2b323e-cluster-a
# back to a stock/default RabbitMQ configuration.
#
# Current (non-default) state captured for rollback:
#   vhosts      : /  /app  /ops          (default = only "/")
#   users       : admin[administrator], app-user[management], ops-user[management]
#                 (default = only "guest" with [administrator])
#   permissions : admin .*/.*/.* on / , /app , /ops
#                 app-user .*/.*/.* on /app ; ops-user .*/.*/.* on /ops
#   policies    : /app  ha-all  (^app-queue$, ha-mode=all, ha-sync-mode=automatic)
#   queues      : /app  app-queue (classic, durable)
#                 /ops  ops-queue (classic, durable)
#
# Running this restores defaults: a single "/" vhost and the stock "guest"
# administrator user, removing all custom vhosts, users, permissions, policies,
# and queues. This destroys configuration the cluster currently depends on —
# only execute during an approved change window.

set -euo pipefail

NS="karma-rabbitmq-maximal-lifecycle-roll-af2b323e-cluster-a"
POD="rabbitmq-0"

rmqctl() { kubectl -n "$NS" exec "$POD" -- rabbitmqctl "$@"; }

echo ">> Removing custom policies"
rmqctl clear_policy -p /app ha-all || true

echo ">> Removing custom queues"
rmqctl delete_queue -p /app app-queue || true
rmqctl delete_queue -p /ops ops-queue || true

echo ">> Clearing custom permissions"
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /app admin     || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /app app-user  || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /ops admin     || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /ops ops-user  || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /   admin      || true

echo ">> Deleting custom vhosts"
rmqctl delete_vhost /app || true
rmqctl delete_vhost /ops || true

echo ">> Deleting custom users"
rmqctl delete_user app-user || true
rmqctl delete_user ops-user || true
rmqctl delete_user admin    || true

echo ">> Restoring stock 'guest' administrator on default vhost '/'"
if ! rmqctl list_users | awk '{print $1}' | grep -qx guest; then
  rmqctl add_user guest guest
fi
rmqctl set_user_tags guest administrator
rmqctl set_permissions -p / guest ".*" ".*" ".*"

echo ">> Rollback to RabbitMQ defaults complete."

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# rollback.sh — REVIEW ARTIFACT (do NOT run against the live cluster yet)
#
# Reverts the RabbitMQ cluster in
#   karma-rabbitmq-maximal-lifecycle-roll-af2b323e-cluster-a
# back to a stock/default RabbitMQ configuration.
#
# Current (non-default) state captured for rollback:
#   vhosts      : /  /app  /ops          (default = only "/")
#   users       : admin[administrator], app-user[management], ops-user[management]
#                 (default = only "guest" with [administrator])
#   permissions : admin .*/.*/.* on / , /app , /ops
#                 app-user .*/.*/.* on /app ; ops-user .*/.*/.* on /ops
#   policies    : /app  ha-all  (^app-queue$, ha-mode=all, ha-sync-mode=automatic)
#   queues      : /app  app-queue (classic, durable)
#                 /ops  ops-queue (classic, durable)
#
# Running this restores defaults: a single "/" vhost and the stock "guest"
# administrator user, removing all custom vhosts, users, permissions, policies,
# and queues. This destroys configuration the cluster currently depends on —
# only execute during an approved change window.

set -euo pipefail

NS="karma-rabbitmq-maximal-lifecycle-roll-af2b323e-cluster-a"
POD="rabbitmq-0"

rmqctl() { kubectl -n "$NS" exec "$POD" -- rabbitmqctl "$@"; }

echo ">> Removing custom policies"
rmqctl clear_policy -p /app ha-all || true

echo ">> Removing custom queues"
rmqctl delete_queue -p /app app-queue || true
rmqctl delete_queue -p /ops ops-queue || true

echo ">> Clearing custom permissions"
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /app admin     || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /app app-user  || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /ops admin     || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /ops ops-user  || true
rmqctl clear_permissions -p /   admin      || true

echo ">> Deleting custom vhosts"
rmqctl delete_vhost /app || true
rmqctl delete_vhost /ops || true

echo ">> Deleting custom users"
rmqctl delete_user app-user || true
rmqctl delete_user ops-user || true
rmqctl delete_user admin    || true

echo ">> Restoring stock 'guest' administrator on default vhost '/'"
if ! rmqctl list_users | awk '{print $1}' | grep -qx guest; then
  rmqctl add_user guest guest
fi
rmqctl set_user_tags guest administrator
rmqctl set_permissions -p / guest ".*" ".*" ".*"

echo ">> Rollback to RabbitMQ defaults complete."
